speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 7th, 1996. The survivor is Boris Chartan. I'm interviewing him. My name is Anne Bernard. The interview is taking place in Plainville, New York, the United States of America. And the language we're using is English. The data is May 7th, 1996. The survivor is Boris Chartan. My name is Anne Bernard. I'm interviewing him in Plainview, New York, the United States of America. The interview is taking place in English. Please tell us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Boris M. Chartan.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does M stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Munir, M-U-N-I-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Chartan? For us?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-A-R-T-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Podkamien, P-O-D-K-A-M-I-E-N, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 17, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Golda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milgron, M-I-L-G-R-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were an only child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your family situation, the house you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a four family house. My grandparents lived with us in one of the apartments. And in the other two apartments, two relatives lived on the opposite side of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who owned the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what your apartment looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It consisted of a kitchen, a living room, and two bedrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have hot and cold running water in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hot water was made by heating it on a stove.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of water? Where did you get it from?

speaker: interviewee
text: The water was in the center of the town. And everybody had to walk up and get their own water, and carry it in. There was no water running into the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who carried the water into the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Either my dad. And there were some people, really, in that town, that made a living for delivering water to the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about indoor plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The plumbing was about a half a block away from the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean an outhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that very inconvenient?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was cold, you had to walk. In the middle of the night, you had to put on a coat to go to the outhouse. It was not easy living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people utilized the outhouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was two houses. Part of it was utilized by us, one. And the second one was utilized by the other family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name was Olaska. And the other family name was Stein, S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were related to you in what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were cousins of my dad's, first cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents lived, you said, in the other apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the other apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chartan, my dad's mom and dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Israel. My grandmother's name was Perl, P-E-R-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: So not all of the families utilized this particular facility outdoors, this outhouse, you're saying, just a few of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the family that lived in those four apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other children in the four apartments besides yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Alaska family had four children. And the Stein family had three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Alaska family was Israel, Mary, and Shoshana, and Junior. The Stein family was Moses, Maere, and Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they near your age, any of the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Junior was closer to my age. The rest were much older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I played with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some of the things you played with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her.

speaker: interviewee
text: With her.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. With Junior, mostly I played as-- as a child, the best I can recollect, is games. As I got a little older, I can remember playing dominoes mostly. We were not-- we did not have the luxury of all the toys. So we just used to make our own little games, and entertain ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember-- can you remember any of the games that you played outside with any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We played a lot of soccer, volleyball. In the winter time, we used to do-- because it's a cold part of the country and the winters last a long time-- we used to do a lot of sled riding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider yourself that you were a good athlete, or a good student, or a combination of both?

speaker: interviewee
text: I consider myself always trying to study a lot, more so than sports.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like most about studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [INAUDIBLE] early childhood at five years. My mom and dad had put me into the cheder already. And the most impression that it made on me was my two teachers. One was Rabbi Akiva. But that's not the Rabbi Akiva that you may think of. But his name was Akiva too. And the other one was Rabbi Israel. And as a child, of course, leaving home, I guess I-- it was difficult. And I cried a lot. But Rabbi Israel, I'll never forget, carried me. He used to pick me up in the house, and carry me to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], really, in his hands as a five-year-old. And I was pulling on his beard most of the time. And I look back on it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hated going back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, after a while I started to like to study, because they were able to convey and relate to me in a manner that made it interesting. Plus, my grandfather would always test me on [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Shabbat what I've learned for the week. And that became a challenge for me, to study, and come home, and to be able to make my grandfather happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a religious family did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a religious family. At that time, practically everybody was, I'd say, religious. Shabbat was Shabbat. Podkamien was Podkamien. There was no such a thing as deviating from the laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish family lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The population of the total Jewish-- not families, but the people, was approximately close to 1,000. The best of my recollection, it's 972 to 1,000 Jews lived Podkamien. At that time, up to the First World War, the population had about 1,400 Jews. And probably about 500 of them must have migrated to America between the First World War and the Second World War, or Israel, both places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- so you mostly spent time with the Jewish children in the neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that age, yes. But at the age of seven, I entered public school. So at that point, the larger population was non-Jewish. So we intermingled with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your earliest recollections of going to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was something a little different than being used to a Podkamien. And I went to cheder at five years, almost a full day, when they put me in. And then I had to split my time to go in the morning to the public school. And the afternoon, when I came out at 2:00, 3 o'clock, I'd spend almost till supper time in cheder. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was difficult, because they had prayer in school. Of course, and the prayer was strictly a Christian prayer. And we had to stand up. But we were recognized that we were not authoring any prayers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So day by day, it became very difficult, because we were outstanding. And they resented it. And I look back today, I could see it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it started breeding a lot of anti-Semitism, which I was not aware at entering school. But in later years, it did come out, because even-- not only were the children resentful. But even the teachers that I had, when I look back some of them were very, very harsh, because I was Jewish. They had to tolerate me in school. And-- but we paid a big price, almost get beat up on the way home from school, and that sort of stuff. It was an ongoing thing. The best that I can remember is I probably must have been already 10 years old. And they played a game where you stand around in a circle-- that's during recess in school. And the teacher would take off his belt, and put it in the back of-- everybody had to keep their hands on their back. And if he put it into you, and if you didn't run fast enough, you got hit. And somehow or other, the belt always never landed in the Jewish kids' hands. And we came home. And we complained. And what is outstanding in my mind is one of the-- my friend's brother, which was a older fellow, had just gotten back from serving in the Polish army. He heard about it. And his brother was in school with me. And he came out to watch this game. And it infuriated him to such a point that he got into a fight with the teacher, tell him that, you know, it's unforgettable to what's going on. And they got into a shoving match. And the teacher had brought him up on charges. The police came. And it wounded up in a trial. But the judge understood that what-- what the teacher did too. And he dismissed the charges against the-- my friend's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that you were more friendly with Jewish children than you were with Christian Polish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: By and large, yes. Yes, because we could relate to them. We could play with them. It was very difficult to be accepted. In rare occasions, you were accepted by a gentile family. My dad had a lot of gentile friends. And that part was good. But, again, these were grown up people. But the kids had their own mind. And-- and they had peer pressure. So I can understand it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does anything else stand out in your mind as very painful about that particular time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Jewish homes were constantly, from time to time, robbed by these villagers. They would come in in the middle of the night, and rob us. I recall a robbery one night that took place in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what was stolen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think what was taken out at that time was some silver that my mother had, and some very expensive bed covers that she had purchased, or was given as a gift, she told me. And that was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the belief of the family that it was an act of anti-Semitism, or just burglars?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was both. It was both. They'd break into Jewish stores at might and rob them. Those days, there was no alarms on the stores. You'd just close up the store the best you can. And at one time, they robbed my dad's store. My dad was a-- sold leather goods. That's how he made his living. We had a store, a leather goods store. He was selling leather for shoemakers, and people to-- mostly-- at that time, there was very little ready made stuff. Mostly, it was made to order. And that's what he was selling, was the leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was robbed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the family's reaction to all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: You just had to take it. And there was no way that the-- you'd make charges, because you were afraid, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that total population of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town probably had, I'd say, about 4,000 total population, Podkamien.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your earliest and happiest memories of any kind of holiday or party that you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The early memories of-- that I could remember was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother's sister Sarah getting married. And my grandfather lived about seven kilometers, my mother's dad, from our home. And that was a smaller village. There was only about 30 Jewish families, maybe, living in that little village. The name of the village was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And the wedding took place there. And it was in the wintertime. And people have gathered from all over the towns. And the-- the-- my uncle came from Duvno, that married my mother's sister. And they were a very recognized family, the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] family. And it was in the winter time. And transportation to get into the village was only by horse and sleigh. And everybody came in the preparation. And they made a very big impression. The ceremony itself-- I mean, the first time I witnessed an occasion. That's the best of my recollection.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember specifically about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The arrival of the rabbi, and his demeanor, and you know, the ceremony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the food that you ate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, the food consisted of [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] it was-- they prepared a chopped meat with eggs inside rolled. And it was sliced. And it was so beautiful and colorful. That's what I can recall, and the rest of the dishes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall the name of that dish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know what they called it. I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your birthday parties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In those days, we were very little birthdays. The-- when I was seven years old, they made me a big birthday party, because I had finished the book of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And I could almost read the whole book. And my grandfather was very proud of it. They made a party on Shabbos. And I read the Torah and shul that Shabbos. Of course, not being Bar Mitzvah yet, I rather it at a junior services. I didn't get [INAUDIBLE] yet. But I was able to read on the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the shuls like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a total of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] four shuls-- four houses of worship. The main house was the shul, which was about 500 feet from the back of our house. That shul was the shul that my grandfather, Israel Chartan, as long as I can remember, all these years he was the president. And the other shul was called the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] shul. They call it a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was a-- because of the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] rabbi, which was very famous, he used to come and stay there. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The other was the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was about a half a block away from the main shul. And the fourth one was the-- called the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was another rabbi, after another rabbi. And in these towns, almost-- quite often, these rabbis would travel through, and spend the Shabbat in the town. And that was also very outstanding with me, because my grandfather would bring me to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was Friday night, and Saturday services. And then also when you do the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which was always outstanding in my mind. And the people enjoyed themselves, and partaking with the rabbi, and studying. And I was a kid. So it was a big impression to watch him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about your relationship with your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close relationship, because he lived so close. My other grandfather, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], lived about seven miles away. I used to see him a lot, but not as often as living in the same house. I remember a lot of Saturdays when the weather was nice, we would walk those seven kilometers after services and Shabbos to go to visit my grandfather. And then at night, we'd be driven home by horse and buggy, like we drove back to our hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a close knit family that you were raised in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider that your father was a successful businessman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, very successful businessman, and had a lot of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of dealings in leather? Did he deal with the general public, or just with people who manufactured products?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with the-- with the people that were making the shoes, and the boots, whatever. But also some would come in, and buy their own, and then take it to the shoemaker to have it made. So he dealt with both.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever help your father in the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to hang around in the store, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish, most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you speak Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, we only spoke if we were out of the house. And among our friends, we'd probably always speak Yiddish. School, or if we went over to a gentile family to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that with any consistency, visit a gentile family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Some of my dad's friends used to come up to stay and spend an hour in our house, have a meal in our house. We did not eat in their houses readily, because of the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. If we'd eat there, we'd have some fruit, some just plain bread, that sort of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother's favorite recipes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My mother's favorite recipes, she would make the cabbage-- stuffed cabbage. She would make [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] stuffed veal [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and the cabbage soup, which I always used for. And she used to make a lot of [? pierogen. ?] This was her famous-- her baking was very good. She used to bake very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever sing to you as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but my mother worked with my father in the store a lot. So I also had a nanny, a Polish lady, which I spent a lot of time. My grandfather from the village brought her up to work in our house so she can take care of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did she sing to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but in Polish. I had to speak Polish to her because she couldn't speak Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- my mother's songs? [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] She used to sing a song [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [YIDDISH], which means on his lap. The words rhyme in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the words?

speaker: interviewee
text: [YIDDISH] That is a song. The rest of the words I-- be probably difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does the melody go?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandpa-- it relates to that his dad has passed away. And he's holding the-- the grandpa is holding the son, and is now teaching him the Kaddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a very sad song.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's a sad song.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever sing any songs like that to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the earliest memories that you have of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war-- the early memories that I have is in-- in 1937, when people started coming towards us from Czechoslovakia. Czechoslovakia was taken. And I used to go to shul regularly, you know, every Shabbos, and even on the weekdays, earl morning services, with my dad and my grandfather.
